metadata.summary: Python project standards: FastAPI patterns with Pydantic, src/ layout, strict type hints with Optional/List/Dict, and uv/poetry lockfile dependency management.

content.title: Python Ecosystem Practices
content.summary: Four foundational Python project conventions: FastAPI with Pydantic models and dependency injection, the standard `src/` project layout, explicit type hints on all public functions, and lockfile-based dependency management via uv (preferred 2026) or poetry.

why.p: Inconsistent Python patterns across projects create fragmentation: agents copy-paste FastAPI snippets from v1 that silently break under v2 Pydantic, project layouts vary per developer making automation scripts non-portable, missing type hints force agents to guess return shapes, and dependency conflicts occur when lockfiles are absent. These four conventions eliminate the most common Python-specific drift.

list.item: Greenfield FastAPI service setup — use as the layout and pattern template.
Refactor passes adding type hints to an existing Python project.
Onboarding a new developer or agent to a Python project — point to this as the house standard.
Dependency conflict troubleshooting — check that a lockfile exists and is committed.
Django-specific patterns — see practices-django-coding for DRF, models, services.
Performance tuning or async architecture decisions — out of scope here.
Projects with an existing house style that diverges (e.g., uses `hatch` instead of uv/poetry) — agent will overwrite it.

rule.statement: FastAPI route handlers MUST use Pydantic BaseModel for request/response bodies, Depends() for injecting DB sessions and auth, and delegate business logic to a service function. Route handlers MUST NOT contain inline DB queries or business logic.
rule.rationale: FastAPI's Depends() system makes auth and DB injection testable via override. Pydantic models provide automatic validation and OpenAPI schema generation. Agents often place logic inline in route handlers when not explicitly constrained.

example.title: FastAPI route with Pydantic and Depends

antipattern.description: Inline DB query inside the route handler body (e.g., `db.query(Item).filter(...).first()` directly in the function).
antipattern.reason: Untestable without the HTTP layer; the same query appears duplicated in other routes or Celery tasks.

rule.statement: Python projects MUST use the `src/` layout: source code under `src/<package>/`, tests under `tests/` at the project root, `pyproject.toml` for metadata, and `.env.example` committed (never `.env`). `conftest.py` lives in `tests/`.
rule.rationale: The `src/` layout prevents the package from being importable from the project root without installation, catching missing `__init__.py` and install issues early. It is the layout recommended by pip, uv, and poetry for 2024+.

example.title: Standard Python project layout
example.code: project/ ├── pyproject.toml ├── src/ │ └── package/ │ ├── __init__.py │ ├── main.py │ └── models/ ├── tests/ │ ├── conftest.py │ └── test_*.py ├── .env.example └── README.md

rule.statement: Every public Python function MUST have complete type annotations: parameter types and return type. Use `from typing import Optional, List, Dict` (Python 3.8 compatible) or native `X | None`, `list[X]`, `dict[K, V]` syntax (Python 3.10+). Bare `def f(x)` with no annotations fails ruff's type-check rules.
rule.rationale: Type hints enable pyright/mypy static analysis, improve agent completions, and serve as inline documentation that cannot drift from the implementation. Missing hints force agents to guess return shapes and produce incorrect downstream code.

example.title: Fully annotated function

antipattern.reason: Breaks static analysis, makes agent completions unreliable, and fails `pyright --strict` checks.

rule.statement: Python projects MUST use `pyproject.toml` for project metadata and configuration. Dependency management MUST use uv (preferred 2026) or poetry — never raw `pip install` without a lockfile. The lockfile (`uv.lock` or `poetry.lock`) MUST be committed. Dev dependencies MUST be separated from runtime dependencies.
rule.rationale: Lockfiles guarantee reproducible installs across developer machines and CI. Without a lockfile, agents install the latest versions which may be incompatible, producing "works on my machine" failures. uv is 10-100x faster than pip and is the standard for 2026.

example.title: uv dependency workflow
example.code:
# Install uv pip install uv
# Create project uv init my-project cd my-project
# Add runtime dependency uv add fastapi
# Add dev dependency uv add --dev pytest ruff mypy
# Install from lockfile (CI) uv sync
# Commit lockfile git add uv.lock pyproject.toml

antipattern.description: Using `pip install -r requirements.txt` without a lockfile, or having both `requirements.txt` and `pyproject.toml` with conflicting versions.
antipattern.reason: Non-locked requirements allow minor version drift that introduces breaking changes silently between installs.

section.name: agent-integration
section.p: Recommended agent workflow for Python projects:

list.item: Pin the dependency manager in the prompt: `uv` (preferred 2026) or `poetry`. Mixed managers in one repo break agent assumptions.
Use `import type { X }` pattern equivalent in Python: `from __future__ import annotations` for forward references before Python 3.10.
Each public function gets a one-line docstring explaining why, not what. Agents default to restating the signature; reject and re-prompt.
Forbid magic numbers/strings — promote to `constants.py`. Agents copy literals across files; lint with `PLR2004` ruff rule.
Agent confuses Pydantic v1 vs v2 syntax (`@validator` vs `@field_validator`) — always state the major version in the prompt.

checklist.check: Project uses src/ layout with pyproject.toml.
Dev dependencies separated from runtime in pyproject.toml.
All public functions have complete type annotations.
FastAPI routes use Depends() for DB/auth injection.
No raw pip install without lockfile in CI.
